system.name: Multi-Agent LLM Threat Modeler
system.description: Threat enriched graph. Three concrete threats identified: LLM prompt injection on if-01, PyPI supply chain substitution on if-03, and insider contributor backdoor on if-04.

subsystems.name: Agent Pipeline
subsystems.description: FrameworkOrchestrator and 9 sequential LLM-driven analysis agents sharing a CanonicalGraph state object.

subsystems.name: LLM Integration Layer
subsystems.description: Adapters that translate agent prompts into calls to remote LLMs (XaiAdapter) or local fixture files (FixtureAdapter).

subsystems.name: Supply Chain and Development Environment
subsystems.description: GitHub repository, PyPI package index, developer workstations (Windows 11 or Linux), and CI pipeline used to build, test, and distribute the tool. Includes all contributors, whose accounts represent a potential insider-threat surface.

components.name: FrameworkOrchestrator
components.description: Sequences all 9 agent stages, enforces schema-validation gates at each boundary, and manages HITL checkpoints.

components.name: Agent Registry and BaseAgent
components.description: Maintains the ordered agent map and dispatches run() calls. BaseAgent handles prompt loading and adapter selection.

components.name: Canonical Graph and State
components.description: In-memory Python dataclass holding the evolving threat model graph (subsystems, components, interfaces, STRIDE, threats, mitigations) passed between agents.

components.name: XaiAdapter
components.description: OpenAI-SDK-compatible HTTPS client targeting the xAI Grok inference API. Reads XAI_API_KEY from the environment at call time.

components.name: FixtureAdapter
components.description: Reads pre-generated JSON/text fixture files from disk instead of making live LLM calls. Used for CI and offline testing.

components.name: GitHub Repository
components.description: Remote Git repository hosting source code, agent prompt files, GitHub Actions workflow definitions, and issue tracking.

components.name: Developer Workstation
components.description: Local machine where the tool is authored, tested, and run. May also serve as the primary deployment host for local threat modeling sessions.

components.name: Python venv and pip
components.description: Isolated Python virtual environment and package installer. Resolves and downloads dependencies from PyPI and executes their setup scripts.

functions.name: Pipeline Execution
functions.description: Sequences all 9 agent stages, validates canonical graph at each boundary, and pauses at HITL gates.

functions.name: LLM Completion
functions.description: Serialises the system-prompt and user-message (architecture JSON), sends them to the xAI Grok API over HTTPS, and returns the LLM response text.

functions.name: Source Code Commit and Push
functions.description: Developer commits code locally and pushes to the GitHub repository over SSH or HTTPS.

functions.name: Dependency Installation
functions.description: Resolves and downloads Python packages from PyPI and executes their setup scripts inside the virtual environment.

interfaces.name: XaiAdapter to xAI Grok API
interfaces.description: HTTPS REST calls from XaiAdapter to the remote xAI Grok inference endpoint. Carries system prompts, user messages containing architecture JSON, and LLM responses. XAI_API_KEY is transmitted as a Bearer token.

stride.S_justification: Compromised XAI_API_KEY allows an attacker to impersonate the tool when calling the Grok API.
stride.T_justification: LLM response content can be adversarially crafted to inject false components or threats into the canonical graph.
stride.R_justification: LLM outputs carry no digital signature; specific responses cannot be attributed to a session.
stride.I_justification: System architecture JSON including component names, interfaces, and trust boundaries is transmitted to a third-party LLM provider.
stride.D_justification: xAI API outage or rate-limiting halts the entire analysis pipeline.
stride.E_justification: Prompt injection in an LLM response could cause agents to produce false security assessments or exfiltrate architecture data.

threats.name: LLM Prompt Injection via Malicious Response Content
threats.description: An adversary who controls or influences the LLM via model poisoning, jailbreak, or API compromise crafts a response that injects false components, threats, or mitigations into the canonical graph, causing the tool to produce a misleading threat model that understates or conceals real risks.

interfaces.name: Developer Workstation to GitHub
interfaces.description: SSH or HTTPS Git operations (push, pull, clone) between the developer workstation and the GitHub-hosted repository. Carries source code, configuration files, and GitHub Actions workflow definitions.

stride.S_justification: Stolen or phished developer credentials allow an attacker to push code as a legitimate developer.
stride.T_justification: Source code could be modified in transit via MITM if HTTPS certificate validation is bypassed.
stride.R_justification: Git commits are attributed by username/email but GPG signing is not enforced on this repository.
stride.I_justification: Repository contents including architecture documents and agent prompts are visible to all contributors.
stride.D_justification: GitHub service outage blocks code push, pull, and CI execution.
stride.E_justification: A compromised developer account could push malicious agent code that appears trusted during PR review.

interfaces.name: pip to PyPI Package Index
interfaces.description: HTTPS requests from pip to PyPI to resolve and download Python packages listed in requirements.txt. Package archives are extracted and setup scripts executed with local user privileges during installation.

stride.S_justification: Dependency confusion and typosquatting allow a malicious package to impersonate a legitimate project dependency.
stride.T_justification: A compromised or malicious PyPI package upload executes arbitrary code during pip install.
stride.R_justification: Package hashes in requirements.txt provide integrity verification when all dependencies are pinned with hashes.
stride.I_justification: Malicious package setup scripts can harvest local environment variables including XAI_API_KEY.
stride.D_justification: PyPI outage or network disruption blocks dependency installation.
stride.E_justification: pip install executes setup.py with full developer-user privileges; a malicious package achieves arbitrary code execution.

threats.name: Supply Chain Compromise via PyPI Dependency Substitution
threats.description: An attacker publishes a malicious package whose name matches or closely resembles a project dependency (typosquatting or dependency confusion). When a developer or CI system runs pip install, the malicious package executes arbitrary code with local user privileges, enabling credential theft or persistent access.

interfaces.name: Contributor to GitHub Repository
interfaces.description: Git push operations from individual contributors (including potential insider threats) to the shared repository. Any contributor with write access can modify source code, agent prompt files, or CI workflow definitions.

stride.S_justification: A compromised or malicious contributor account appears as a trusted identity in git history.
stride.T_justification: A malicious contributor can inject backdoors into agent logic, LLM adapter code, or GitHub Actions workflow definitions.
stride.R_justification: PR review history and git log provide a partial audit trail but can be bypassed through social engineering of reviewers.
stride.I_justification: Contributors with repository access can read all source code and any secrets accidentally committed.
stride.D_justification: An insider could corrupt branches, but GitHub branch-protection rules limit the blast radius.
stride.E_justification: A contributor with write access to the main branch (if branch protection is not enforced) can introduce supply chain backdoors.

threats.name: Insider Threat: Malicious Code Injection via Contributor Access
threats.description: A malicious or compromised contributor with repository write access injects backdoor code into agent logic, the LLM adapter, or CI workflow definitions. The change may be disguised as a routine bug fix or feature addition and pass superficial code review, compromising every downstream consumer of the tool.
